attributes.stem: A 28-year-old man is admitted to the Intensive Care Unit following a high-speed motor vehicle accident. His pulse rate is 50/min, blood pressure 80/45 mmHg, respiratory rate 10/min, SpO2 98% on mechanical ventilation, and temperature 36.5°C. His peripheries are warm and dry with brisk capillary refill, and he has a large open wound across his neck and chest.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with neurogenic shock, which typically occurs following a high spinal cord injury. The key features in this case are hypotension paired with bradycardia, along with warm, dry skin. This distinguishes his presentation from other causes of shock.

Neurogenic shock occurs due to the sudden loss of sympathetic tone following a spinal cord injury. This results in vasodilation below the level of injury (causing hypotension) and unopposed vagal tone (causing bradycardia). Warm, dry skin below the level of injury is also a hallmark of neurogenic shock, distinguishing it from other forms of shock.

Another important differential in the trauma patient is hypovolemic shock. This would be characterised by the hypotensive patient with tachycardia and cold, clammy skin. While this patient is hypotensive, the presence of bradycardia and warm peripheries makes hypovolemic shock less likely than neurogenic shock.

Obstructive shock, such as from cardiac tamponade or tension pneumothorax, typically presents with hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnoea and/or hypoxia depending on the underlying cause. The lack of specific symptoms related to these conditions make obstructive shock unlikely.

Cardiogenic shock typically presents with hypotension, tachycardia, and signs of poor peripheral perfusion such as cold, clammy skin.

Septic shock is characterised by hypotension, tachycardia, and often fever. While patients with septic shock can have warm skin due to vasodilation, the presence of bradycardia in an otherwise well 28-year-old makes this diagnosis unlikely. It is also a less likely diagnosis in a trauma patient.
learningPoints: Neurogenic shock is characterized by hypotension and bradycardia due to loss of sympathetic tone following spinal cord injury.
Neurogenic shock is distinguished from other types of shock by the presence of warm, dry skin below the level of injury.
Neurogenic shock typically occurs with injuries at or above T6 level of the spinal cord.
references: Grigorean VT, Sandu AM, Popescu M, Iacobini MA, Stoian R, Neascu C, Strambu V, Popa F. Cardiac dysfunctions following spinal cord injury. J Med Life. 2009 Apr-Jun;2(2):133-45. PMID: 20108532; PMCID: PMC3018985.

Dave S, Dahlstrom JJ, Weisbrod LJ. Neurogenic Shock. [Updated 2023 Oct 29]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459361/
